Brent bears build near-record short position on peace memo, shifting balance of risks

Investors have become very bearish about the outlook for crude oil prices following the conclusion of a memorandum of understanding between the United States and Iran to terminate their war and re-open the Strait of Hormuz.

Hedge funds and other money managers sold the equivalent of 95 million barrels in ICE Brent futures and options over the seven days ending on June 16 (a period that included announcement of the memorandum).

Fund managers sold Brent at the fastest rate for more than a year, and at one of the fastest weekly rates since the coronavirus pandemic ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to subscribers earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

Drinking from the cup of poison: White House reluctantly catches up with the oil market

Oil traders and hedge funds increasingly anticipated the imminent re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz for more than a month before the United States and Iran concluded a memorandum of understanding ending their war on June 15.

With petroleum inventories at home running down rapidly, top U.S. policymakers faced an increasingly urgent deadline to decide whether to reach an agreement with Iran or attempt to re-open the Strait fully by force. 

Ultimately, the White House seems to have decided the embarrassment of a diplomatic deal achieving less than its stated war aims was a lesser evil than the potential risks of trying to force the Strait open against hostile fire. 

In recent weeks, the threats, blockades, clandestine convoys and attacks on missile and drone facilities near the waterway can all be seen as an effort to escape making this unpleasant choice. 

But after exploring various options, the White House appears to have concluded none of the military ones was acceptable and it would have to settle for a deal achieving much less than it initially hoped when the war started.  

In reaching that decision, the White House was catching up with a conclusion many oil traders and fund managers had reached weeks earlier ...

As Traders Avoid Risk, Stocks Fall & Dollar Rises - #Asia #MarketWrap 
Global equities edged lower in holiday-thinned trading as investors assessed whether a 60-day diplomatic window between the US and Iran could pave the way for a broader nuclear agreement and a more durable peace settlement.

MSCI's global equity index fell 0.2%, trimming weekly gains, while an Asian stock gauge slipped 0.9% after a five-day rally that had carried the benchmark to record highs. S&P 500 futures declined 0.5% following Thursday's 1.1% advance, and the Dollar strengthened against most major peers as investors adopted a more cautious stance heading into the weekend. Market liquidity was reduced by holiday closures across the US, China, Hong Kong and Taiwan.

Brent crude traded below $80/bbl and was on course for a weekly decline of roughly 9%. The sharp drop reflects easing concerns over global energy supplies after the US-Iran interim peace agreement helped restore shipping activity through the Strait of Hormuz, unwinding much of the conflict-related risk premium in oil markets.

Bond yields moved higher across parts of the Asia-Pacific region, with 10-year Japanese and Australian yields advancing. In the US, two-year Treasury yields steadied around 4.18% after surging 13 bps in the previous session to their highest level in more than a year as traders increased bets on future Fed tightening following the central bank's hawkish hold.

Gold was on track for a third consecutive weekly loss as expectations for higher interest rates outweighed optimism surrounding the peace agreement. Adding to the pressure, Goldman Sachs cut its year-end gold price forecast by $500/oz, reflecting a less supportive outlook for the precious metal amid rising real yields and a stronger Dollar.

🚨 Oil Producers Say “Never Again” to Strait of Hormuz — Structural Shift After Conflict

Even after the US-Iran ceasefire reopens the Strait, oil-producing countries are sticking with alternative routes.

Goldman Sachs forecast:
• Pre-war 100% → Post-war ~70% traffic levels
• Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq ramping up bypass pipelines & new ports
• UAE officially declares “Zero dependence on Hormuz”

After 107 days of blockade, Gulf nations built permanent alternatives they’re not abandoning.

This could reshape global oil supply chains and energy security long-term.

For import-dependent countries like Korea, this is a wake-up call on energy diversification.

What do you think — how will this impact global oil prices and energy markets going forward? 👇

BRENT CRUDE IS UP TO 80$ AFTER IRAN CLOSED THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ! https://t.co/MtCxjkb3Gx

The war on Iran has cost American households 132 billion dollars and Iran several thousand lives, and a 60 day ceasefire window is the only thing separating that total from the next one.

Moody's Analytics, led by chief economist Mark Zandi, puts the cost to U.S. consumers and taxpayers at 132 billion dollars so far. The largest share moved through the price at the pump, with Brent crude topping 110 dollars a barrel during the conflict. Zandi calculated the household-level hit at roughly 750 dollars per family, driven by military spending and oil disruption across the Middle East. The Pentagon funded the operation out of its existing operations and maintenance budget, meaning a supplemental spending request to Congress is still coming.

The bill abroad:

Iran's death toll sits above 3,300, including 170 killed in a strike attributed to a Tomahawk missile hitting a girls' school. Several thousand more have died in Lebanon amid Israel's parallel offensive, with dozens killed in Israel and across Gulf states. The memorandum ending active combat includes a 300 billion dollar reconstruction package for Iran, a figure that dwarfs the cost Moody's assigns to American households. Moody's has issued no new forecast for Iran, citing exceptionally high uncertainty.

What the war bought:

Global energy markets absorbed the shock directly, with a key shipping waterway disrupted for weeks and fuel rationing reported across parts of Asia and Africa. Supply chains for semiconductors and fertilizer felt the strain alongside oil, and several Middle Eastern economies took a harder hit than U.S. figures capture. Inflation and mortgage rates moved at home, complicating the early agenda of incoming Federal Reserve chief Kevin Warsh.

My take: A state that tracks its own war spending in household grocery money while pricing the country it bombed in reconstruction billions has told you whose suffering counts as data.

The 132 billion dollar figure is real, but it exists as a headline number only by treating gas prices as the unit of measurement for a war that killed thousands of people who never appear in a Moody's report. The 300 billion dollar reconstruction commitment is the more honest price tag, an admission buried in diplomatic language of the actual scale of destruction. Energy markets and defense contractors moved the real money upward long before any household felt the 750 dollar pinch, and that flow is the part no ceasefire negotiation will touch.

The Strait of Hormuz: Iran's Ultimate Leverage  And America's Defining Choice

Iran has done it again. They have violated the sanctity of the Strait of Hormuz, choked global oil flows, and proven that this narrow waterway is their most devastating weapon. Make no mistake: Tehran understands its leverage. By threatening the strait, Iran can hold the world economy hostage spiking energy prices, fueling inflation, and destabilizing markets from Tokyo to New York.

The math is brutal. When Iran flexed its muscles, Brent crude surged toward $120 a barrel. Global inflation hit 4%. Growth slowed to a meager 2.5%  the weakest in years. Jet fuel prices doubled. Fertilizer and aluminum markets buckled. The message was clear: Iran doesn't need a nuclear bomb to cripple the West. It just needs a choke point.

And yet, the ceasefire deal that reopened the strait handed Tehran a staggering victory $300 billion in reconstruction aid and sanctions relief. That is not diplomacy. That is ransom paid under duress.

So now America faces a singular, unforgiving choice one that will define its superpower status for generations:

Option 1: Accept defeat by stalemate.

We pretend the war ended in a draw. We manage a hostile, nuclear-ambitious Iran that now knows its leverage works. We allow a powerful, emboldened regime to reshape the Middle East in its image. This is not peace. This is slow-motion surrender.

Option 2: Win unconditionally.

That means military action decisive enough to destroy Iran's nuclear and missile programs, dismantle its proxy network, and break its grip on the strait permanently. It means ground operations if necessary, and accepting the short-term economic pain for long-term security. It means sending an unmistakable signal: the United States does not bargain with its lifelines.

There is no third path. A half-measure airstrikes without follow-through, sanctions without teeth only invites Iranian retaliation and emboldens its allies in Moscow and Beijing. Withdrawal would hand the region to chaos, pushing Saudi Arabia and others toward their own nuclear programs and igniting an arms race no one can control.

We win, or we lose. That is the brutal arithmetic. Iran has made its play. The Strait of Hormuz is no longer just a maritime passage it is a battlefield of wills. And America, like it or not, must decide if it still has the will to prevail.

Unconditional surrender from Tehran, or unconditional acceptance of Iranian power.
The choice is ours. History will not forgive a middle ground.

LIVE NEWS REPORT
Saturday, June 20, 2026
1. U.S.-IRAN DEAL TAKES EFFECT, QUESTIONS REMAIN
The United States and Iran have signed a memorandum of
understanding intended to end the war, reopen the Strait of Hormuz,
and move both sides toward a final agreement within 60 days.
The agreement includes Iranian steps on highly enriched uranium,
limited sanctions relief, and free passage through Hormuz for now.
Pakistan and Qatar helped mediate.
Who is affected: shipping companies, energy markets, Gulf states,
Iranian civilians, U.S. forces, and regional allies.
Why it matters: Hormuz is one of the world’s most important oil and
gas routes. A stable agreement could lower the danger of wider war.
What is next: U.S.-Iran talks are expected to continue through Swiss
and Qatari channels.
Still unknown: whether the agreement will hold, how Iran will verify
nuclear steps, and how Israel, Hezbollah, and other regional actors
will respond.
2. LEBANON CEASEFIRE STRAINED BY DEADLY STRIKES
Israeli strikes in southern Lebanon killed at least 16 people after
a reported ceasefire with Hezbollah. Israel said it was responding
to Hezbollah fire. Hezbollah said it remains committed to the truce
while opposing Israeli presence in Lebanese territory.
Who is affected: civilians in southern Lebanon and northern Israel,
Lebanese emergency workers, displaced families, and regional
diplomats.
Why it matters: the Lebanon front could damage wider U.S.-Iran
diplomacy and renew a larger regional war.
What is next: mediators are trying to keep the ceasefire alive.
Still unknown: whether both sides will halt fire long enough for
talks to proceed.
3. GAZA DEATHS RENEW CONCERN OVER CEASEFIRE CONDITIONS
Palestinian health officials said Israeli fire killed people in Gaza,
including children and women. Israel did not immediately give a full
public account of the specific incidents.
Who is affected: Gaza civilians, Israeli soldiers, hostages’ families,
aid groups, and mediators.
Why it matters: the Gaza ceasefire remains fragile, with continued
deaths and no final settlement over disarmament, withdrawal, and
security arrangements.
What is next: pressure continues for humanitarian access and a wider
political agreement.
Still unknown: whether negotiators can move past temporary pauses
toward a lasting settlement.
4. UKRAINE SEEKS MORE HELP AFTER G7 TALKS
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said G7 leaders pledged more
help for Ukraine against Russia. The focus includes air defense,
energy protection, sanctions pressure, and support for Ukraine’s
defense industry.
Who is affected: Ukrainian civilians, soldiers, energy workers, and
European allies.
Why it matters: Ukraine’s energy grid and cities remain targets as
Russia’s invasion continues.
What is next: Ukraine will seek more weapons, air-defense missiles,
and European backing.
Still unknown: how quickly promised aid will arrive and whether new
sanctions will change Russia’s military decisions.
5. U.S. COURTS AND GOVERNMENT: TRANSPARENCY AND CONSUMER WATCHDOG
A U.S. Senate panel advanced bipartisan bills that would allow cameras
in Supreme Court and some lower federal court proceedings. Supporters
say the public should see more of the justice system. Opponents raise
security and trial fairness concerns.
Separately, a federal appeals court blocked immediate Trump
administration plans to cut roughly two-thirds of the Consumer
Financial Protection Bureau workforce.
Who is affected: court observers, federal judges, consumers, financial
firms, and agency workers.
Why it matters: both stories concern public trust in institutions:
how courts are seen and how consumer complaints are handled.
What is next: court-camera bills still need fuller congressional
action. CFPB staffing litigation continues.
Still unknown: whether either court-access bill can pass both chambers.
6. TEXAS STORMS BRING FLOODING AND POWER OUTAGES
Overnight storms in the San Antonio area caused flash flooding and
widespread outages. Local reports said more than 17,000 CPS Energy
customers were without power Saturday morning.
The National Weather Service warned that heavy rain could flood
creeks, roads, highways, and underpasses. Residents were told not to
drive through flooded roads.
Who is affected: San Antonio families, drivers, utility crews,
emergency responders, and people without air conditioning.
Why it matters: flooding and heat can become dangerous together,
especially for older adults, children, and people with medical needs.
What is next: crews will keep restoring power as weather allows.
Still unknown: the full damage total and how long every outage will
last.
7. EUROPE HEATWAVE PROMPTS EMERGENCY RESPONSE
France called crisis talks as a major heatwave spread across parts of
Europe. Forecasts pointed to temperatures near 105°F in parts of
France, while Germany and Italy also faced dangerous heat.
Who is affected: older adults, outdoor workers, tourists, people
without cooling, farmers, and energy systems.
Why it matters: early-season heat can overwhelm health systems and
raise wildfire, storm, and power-demand risks.
What is next: officials may extend cooling measures, park hours,
public alerts, and health guidance.
Still unknown: how long the worst heat will last and whether storms
will follow in the hardest-hit areas.
8. FATAL TRAIN CRASH NORTH OF LONDON
A collision between two passenger trains near Bedford killed one train
driver and injured many others. Nine people remained in critical
condition Saturday.
British Transport Police and rail investigators are examining the
cause. The trains were bound for London and operated by East Midlands
Railway.
Who is affected: passengers, rail staff, families of the injured, and
commuters.
Why it matters: the crash raises urgent safety questions for one of
Britain’s busy rail corridors.
What is next: investigators will examine signals, train movement,
crew actions, equipment, and track conditions.
Still unknown: the exact cause of the collision.
9. PAKISTAN-ADMINISTERED KASHMIR SHUTDOWN FOLLOWS DEADLY UNREST
A shutdown in Pakistan-administered Kashmir has disrupted daily life
after protests left more than 20 people dead over two weeks.
The unrest is tied to anger over political representation and planned
regional election seats. Authorities have used road closures, arrests,
and internet restrictions.
Who is affected: civilians, police, taxi drivers, shopkeepers, day
laborers, and families unable to move freely.
Why it matters: the crisis shows how political disputes can quickly
harm ordinary workers and families.
What is next: authorities face pressure to restore order and address
grievances.
Still unknown: whether protest leaders and officials will reach a
settlement.
10. HEALTH: EBOLA FUNDING AND H5N1 IN AUSTRALIA
The CDC activated $107 million in emergency funding for Ebola response
connected to outbreaks in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda.
The response includes surveillance, diagnostics, isolation, and safe
burial work.
Australia confirmed its first mainland H5N1 bird-flu case in a
seabird near Esperance, Western Australia. Officials said poultry and
agriculture had not yet been affected.
Who is affected: communities in central Africa, health workers,
travel-screening teams, Australian wildlife officials, farmers, and
coastal ecosystems.
Why it matters: Ebola can spread rapidly without strong containment.
H5N1 can devastate birds and disrupt food systems.
What is next: disease monitoring, testing, and biosecurity measures
will expand.
Still unknown: the full scale of the Ebola outbreak and whether H5N1
will spread to farms or more wildlife.
11. TECHNOLOGY: TELEGRAM LOSES INDIA COURT CHALLENGE
Telegram lost a bid to overturn India’s temporary order blocking the
app during a key medical school exam. A New Delhi court ruled the
government action was legal and reasonable for exam integrity.
Who is affected: students, exam officials, Telegram users, tech firms,
and digital-rights advocates.
Why it matters: governments are testing how far they can restrict
platforms during exams, elections, and security events.
What is next: technology companies may face more temporary access
orders in high-stakes public events.
Still unknown: how often courts will approve similar restrictions.
12. RELIGION: U.S. CATHOLIC BISHOPS GATHER IN ORLANDO
U.S. Catholic bishops met in Orlando and consecrated the nation to
the Sacred Heart. The gathering comes during a season of public debate
over faith, culture, national identity, and church leadership.
Who is affected: Catholic clergy, lay believers, schools, charities,
and local parishes.
Why it matters: religious institutions continue to shape moral,
educational, and public-service life across the country.
What is next: bishops and dioceses will carry decisions and prayer
emphases back to local communities.
Still unknown: how much public impact the gathering will have beyond
Catholic institutions.
13. CULTURE AND HUMAN INTEREST: OBAMA CENTER OPENS IN CHICAGO
Barack and Michelle Obama surprised early visitors at the opening of
the Obama Presidential Center in Chicago. The opening coincided with
Juneteenth and included public spaces, a museum, a library branch,
play areas, and community facilities.
Who is affected: Chicago residents, visitors, students, historians,
and the South Side community.
Why it matters: presidential centers can become civic, educational,
and cultural gathering places.
What is next: public demand is high, with tickets reportedly sold out
well ahead.
Still unknown: the long-term effect on neighborhood traffic, tourism,
and local businesses.
14. EDUCATION: FEDERAL INVESTIGATIONS IN MICHIGAN DISTRICTS
The U.S. Department of Education opened three investigations into
Michigan school districts over alleged Title IX violations.
Who is affected: students, parents, school leaders, teachers, and
district legal teams.
Why it matters: Title IX disputes affect school policy, student
rights, athletics, privacy, and discipline.
What is next: federal investigators will seek documents and responses
from the districts.
Still unknown: whether the investigations will lead to findings,
settlements, or policy changes.
15. HOPEFUL DEVELOPMENT: U.S. ADVANCES IN WORLD CUP
The United States defeated Australia 2-0 and advanced to the 2026
World Cup knockout round, winning its group.
Who is affected: players, fans, host cities, broadcasters, and local
businesses around tournament sites.
Why it matters: a strong U.S. run increases national attention on the
sport during a home-soil World Cup.
What is next: the U.S. will play in the round of 32 on July 1 in
Santa Clara, California.
Still unknown: whether injured players will return and how far the
team can advance.

OIL IS ABOUT TO GO PARABOLIC https://t.co/grWhlinAhz

Tensions in the Middle East escalated today as Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, potentially disrupting global oil supply chains and energy markets. This move comes amid reported violations of a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with the US and Israel. The closure could exacerbate geopolitical tensions in the region.

🚨 Iran–U.S. MOU Tensions Escalate

Weekend developments are raising questions about the durability of the Iran–U.S. agreement:

🔴 Iran's Joint Military Command announced the Strait of Hormuz is closed to vessel traffic, citing alleged U.S. and Israeli ceasefire violations.

🟢 U.S. Vice President JD Vance pushed back, saying there is "no evidence" the Strait is actually being closed and that negotiations remain constructive.

🤝 U.S. officials say talks are progressing, with another round of discussions potentially taking place in Switzerland on Sunday.

🇮🇷 Iran's Foreign Ministry confirmed it will send a delegation to Switzerland, but struck a tougher tone:

• "We will demand implementation of U.S. commitments"
• "Commitment for commitment"
• Warned Iran will take "necessary measures" if the other side fails to comply
📈 Oil futures initially spiked on the Strait headlines before giving back much of the move.
🎯 Key takeaway:

The deal remains alive, but both sides are now publicly accusing the other of falling short on commitments, increasing uncertainty around the ceasefire and the future of the MOU.
@anasalhajji But, and correct me if I'm wrong, don't commercial crude inventories usually build between end of Feb-middle of June?  https://t.co/gkmuTyG3yC

Get this: 

🌀Since the start of the war on Iran until now, OECD commercial crude inventories have declined by just 8.37 million barrels.

🌀If you exclude the US, they’ve actually gone up.  Yet, they keep telling you "GLOBAL" inventories are being depleted! 

🟥Again, don’t be fooled by charts that lump commercial crude, SPR, and product inventories all together.

Near-record speculative shorts on Brent while US crude inventories sit at their lowest since 1985, Hormuz traffic is still at roughly 20% of pre-war baseline, and Qatar's Ras Laffan is offline for 3-5 years. The asymmetry runs one way: short-covering happens in hours, physical supply normalization takes months to years. Every structural data point is pointing opposite the current positioning.

The physical market gives a different read. US crude inventories are at their lowest since 1985, yet WTI calendar spreads haven't reflected that inventory tightness the way you'd expect. OPEC+ is simultaneously rolling back quota cuts while holding over 5 million barrels per day of spare capacity — so the price signal is navigating both a geopolitical risk reprice and a structural supply overhang. The divergence between physical tightness and financial price usually resolves in favor of the inventory signal, but the spare capacity buffer is what makes this cycle unusual.

@marcijohnsen @BulwarkOnline @JVLast That’s a difficult question. I would not have started the war in the first place. Trump is racing to get something done in the short run bc of the midterms &  bc he knows we are soon to run out of crude inventories globally which would cause gas prices to jump again.

This is the official story from the US Government, the EIA:

US Commercial Crude Oil Inventories were drawn down by -8.26m barrels for the week ending June 12th.  This brought US Commercial Crude inventories to 418.2m barrels.

The average draw over the last four weeks is -6.71m.

If we assume a weekly draw of 6.71m barrels for the week ending today, June 19th, that would bring commercial inventories down to 411.5m barrels.

The operational floor is said to be around 350m barrels.  Oil in pipelines can’t be drained, the pipelines require hydraulic pressure.  Sludge in the bottom of tanks, etc.

This leaves US Commercial Crude Inventories at just 61.5m barrels.  With an average draw of -6.7m barrels per week, the US has just nine weeks left.

Iran just closed the Strait again.  JD Vance has cancelled his trip to Switzerland to sign a peace agreement with Iran.  The clock is ticking.  Brent short interest is at a 5-year high.

At what point do the markets panic?  8 weeks?  7 weeks?  6 weeks?  It could be any day now.  Cushing was at the operational minimum inventory level of 20m barrels as of June 12th.

The Syrian Ministry of Defense announced today, Saturday, the death of two of its soldiers in an attack launched by unidentified gunmen riding a motorcycle near the city of Manbij, northeast of Aleppo.

A similar attack targeted a Ministry of Defense transport bus two days ago, carried out by unidentified individuals on the Tal Tamer - Ras al-Ayn road in the Al-Hasakah countryside, resulting in one death and several injuries.

Meanwhile, the Ministry of Interior announced last week the death of an internal security force member and the injury of three others in a suicide attack by the terrorist organization ISIS in the city of Raqqa, northeast of the country.

The activity of the terrorist organization ISIS has been escalating recently, with fingers pointing at it for such attacks. The group has claimed responsibility for several operations, including targeting an Iraqi oil tanker in Manbij and planting an explosive device in the car of the head of the Palace of Justice department in the town of Babbila, south of Damascus, which exploded in the Daf al-Shawk neighborhood.

In the Raqqa attack, the organization referred to one of the perpetrators as "Al-Muhajir" (The Emigrant), indicating that the operative is a foreigner. Furthermore, the group is rallying its followers to attack oil tankers, especially Iraqi ones, as part of what it calls an "economic war" in its latest publication.

🇦🇷🇧🇷🛢️ Argentina and Brazil will lead Latin American oil growth until 2050.

According to OPEC projections, Latin American oil production will grow from 7.5 million barrels per day (mbd) in 2025 to 11.6 mbd in 2050, driven mainly by both countries.

The main contribution to LatAm growth will come from Brazil , which will continue to expand its production through developments in ultra-deep waters, especially in the pre-salt.

OPEC estimates that the country will go from producing ~3.7 mbd in 2025 to 4.4 mbd in 2030, to reach a peak of close to 5.8 mbd during the 2040s.

The other major player will be Argentina, with Vaca Muerta as the central driver of growth. 

OPEC projects that national production will grow from ~1 mbd in 2025 to 1.3 mbd in 2030, reaching close to 2.1 mbd by mid-century.

The projected production growth will allow LatAm to significantly increase its crude oil exports. 

Shipments abroad will grow from 4.5 mbd in 2025 to 7.4 mbd in 2050, with an estimated peak of 7.7 mbd around 2045 and Asia as the main destination.

We are beginning to see how Iran will utilize their newfound power in the region, and I suspect that oil bulls will be quite happy with the results. I’ve talked about the geopolitics quite a bit, but other than referencing a general rise in oil price, I haven’t spoken on the economic power that they will yield.

Previously, OPEC largely controlled the price of oil as it managed the spigot. Sure, there are a multitude of other factors, but they played the largest role in my opinion. 

The UAE and Qatar no longer belong to that organization, and it will likely become a shell of its former self. Now that Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz, they will have overall say in the flow of that oil, minimally for the next year or until overland pipelines can be completed.

So, with that in mind, what oil price would Iran like to see? I think this is an exceedingly important question for those of us investing in oil, particularly for the near to long-term.

Iran needs Brent to maintain approximately $124-$125 a barrel to balance its budget. This would be the most basic of targets, and I suspect it’s likely their minimum price. Besides budgetary concerns, there is also the obvious interest in placing economic strain on the United States. President Trump, very stupidly imo, announced to the world that the US is at critical levels of oil reserve capacities.

So, if $124-$125 per barrel is their base case, at what price could Iran place economic strain on the United States, while also refraining from collapsing the global economy? It should be heavily noted that China will not want to see widespread economic collapse. They will have no problem with financial strain upon the US system, however. 

That target, ladies and gentlemen, is likely $130-$140 per barrel. A spike to 150 to 160 might teeter the global economy over the edge, and they may spike it for a day or two for demonstration purposes; however sustained levels will likely be at the $130 to $140 range.

Without a doubt, they will be capable of influencing prices in the direction of their choosing. Because of our critical SPR levels, they will maintain this ability for the next year. The Permian Basin along with the Alaskan fields will see great expansion over this period. Eventually, their control will wane, but not anytime soon in my opinion. DD and GLTY. NFA as always. There are obviously innumerous factors at play, and things are changing by the minute. I’m making largely predictive statements, and could very well be wrong; however, if things continue in the current fashion, I suspect I will be quite correct.

1. Today’s lesson in katanomics is brought to you from the Ghana-Ivory Coast border, where the two countries have decided, once again, to revive the idea of an OPEC for cocoa, or "COPEC."

2. OPEC is an oil cartel envisaged by Venezuelan leaders in the 1940s and successfully sold to Arab sheikhs in the 1950s.

3. [Side note: at its founding in 1960, it chose "neutral" Geneva as its HQ and neutral English as the only working language. The stubborn refusal of the Swiss to grant some diplomatic tax & other benefits led OPEC to move the HQ to Vienna in 1965. Neither Austria nor Switzerland is a member.]

4. Everyone knows OPEC as a club of major oil-producing countries that sets adjustable quotas for its members so that the club can tweak total oil flow into the global market and thus fix prices.

5. The truth is more complex.

6. At its founding in 1960, OPEC accounted for 40% of global oil production. When Arab oil sheikhs decided to flex their muscles and shake the global economy in 1973, the oil shock happened. At that time, OPEC hit its peak control of 50% of global output.

7. Today, OPEC controls less than 37% of global oil output.

8. That is why Ghanaian and Ivorian strategists have been chasing the COPEC dream for more than a decade now. If the two countries produce 65% of the world's cocoa, then why can't they flex their chocolatey muscle too?

9. Here is why I said the OPEC analogy is complex.

10. OPEC is no longer very effective in controlling prices over the medium term. Saudi Arabia and OPEC ally Russia have the most means to adjust short-term output. That can hit prices. But if they play too long, non-OPEC producers in the US & elsewhere simply step up production when prices rise due to the shortages forcing the price level back down.

11. Secondly, OPEC members game their quotas. There is no central enforcement mechanism. And some of the countries have fiscal indiscipline issues that just make it hard for them.

12. All the above simply tells us that commodity cartels are simple political answers but complex and confusing policy solutions, which is the central point of katanomics. It is easier to set a political vision that most people can rally behind than to craft a policy response that survives contact with reality.

13. Ghana’s strategic blueprint for cocoa sees expansion from ~600k tonnes today to 1.5 million tonnes in coming years. How does one control volumes when expansion is the primary policy objective? In a context where Ecuador has taken over from Ghana as the 2nd largest producer and chocolate manufacturers are using more substitutes for cocoa?

14. Ghana has worse cost factors than Ivory Coast because virgin land, migrant labour, and higher commercialisation favour Ivory Coast. Since 2003, Ghana has paid farmers more for cocoa and yet struggled to meet its volume targets. Rather than research more deeply, Ghana simply blames smuggling to Ivory Coast (see detailed piece in the comments).

15. How will quota enforcement work with all this confusion?

16. Furthermore, OPEC oil ministers have more direct control over oil production because state-owned companies produce a lot of oil and oil production can be modulated by simply turning valves on and off.

17. Cocoa production is highly fragmented among millions of small-scale farmers in West Africa. Quota fixing within short timescales is simply impractical.

18. The fact is that Ghana & Ivory Coast have tried to flex market power recent years by adding a $400 surcharge on cocoa called LID.

19. Buyers simply adjusted Ghana's quality premium downwards by $540 to offset the surcharge, leaving farmers worse off by $140 a tonne.

20. In short, when you say "we control 65% of the world's cocoa so our farmers ought to benefit more," you are rallying people behind a political vision about the "desirable WHAT." The policy constraint is HOW you do it by managing multiple trade-offs. That is the lesson of katanomics.

Managed Money, June 20, 2026

The macro landscape completely fractured this week. President Donald Trump signed an interim ceasefire agreement at the G7 summit in France, bringing a sudden halt to the four-month maritime siege. The 14-point memorandum of understanding establishes an immediate 60-day negotiation window, completely lifts the U.S. naval blockade on Iranian ports, and authorizes a toll-free reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. 

Though President Trump warned on social media that the U.S. would resume dropping bombs if Tehran fails to behave during Swiss implementation talks, the physical oil supply outlook changed overnight. Concurrently, the Federal Reserve delivered a separate shockwave during Chairman Kevin Warsh's debut FOMC meeting. 

While keeping benchmark rates steady at 3.5% to 3.75%, Warsh radically streamlined Fed communications by slashing forward guidance and entirely dropping easing-leaning language. More critically, the updated dot plot which notably omitted Warsh's personal submission revealed a highly hawkish committee shift, with a clear majority of policymakers now projecting interest rate hikes before year-end to fight 4.2% inflation. 

This dual hammer of an impending OPEC oil flood and a tightening Fed triggered an absolute liquidation wave, driving July WTI Crude to plunge hard down toward $73.00/bbl and forcing a major cross-asset risk recalibration.

Commitments of Traders - Managed Money Positions Overview

Report Date: June 19, 2026 | Data Period: Positions as of June 16, 2026

The Smart Money vs. Dumb Money Metric

Commercial end-users and major agribusiness giants are viewing the dramatic drop in energy prices as a massive operational gift, aggressively locking in cheaper fuel and freight inputs while holding their ground on structural grain longs. Institutional desks are holding core physical asset positions, betting that a 60-day geopolitical truce cannot easily erase structural supply deficits.

Fund managers are in a state of absolute panic and forced liquidation, frantically unwinding their long energy premium and covering macro shorts in financials to adjust to the reality of Kevin Warsh's hawkish Fed.

Small retail speculators are heavily shorting the fixed income market, completely caught off guard by the hawkish dot plot shift right as the macro backdrop shifts beneath them.

Precious Metals & Financials (The Warsh Era Begins)

Gold (COMEX): 112,800 (Net Long) | Δ -300 | Current ~$4,615
Gold faced moderate liquidation pressure as the dollar caught an intense hawkish bid following the Fed meeting. Despite the near-term volatility, commercial buyers are stepping in above $4,600, recognizing that structural core inflation remains hot at 4.2% and won't vanish with a temporary Middle East ceasefire.

Silver (COMEX): 10,400 (Net Long) | Δ -500 | Current ~$71.85
Speculative length softened as funds reduced broader commodity exposure. Industrial buyers are stepping back out of immediate panic-buying mode on expectations that the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz will eventually clear global manufacturing logistics backlogs by late summer.

10-Year Note: -1,815,000 | Δ +30,000 (Leveraged Funds)
The Hawkish Gavel. Leveraged funds were forced into modest short-covering as the 10-year yield surged on the Fed's aggressive tone. Warsh shocked the street by delivering a vastly shortened policy statement focused purely on price stability, signaling an era of data-dependent tightening.

Energy & Natural Gas
WTI Crude: 168,500 (Net Long) | Δ -11,300 | Support $70.00 | Resist $85.00
The Blockade Breaker. Managed money executed an absolute fire sale, throwing WTI down to the $73.00/bbl handle following the signed U.S.-Iran treaty. The premium has rapidly evaporated, but the physical market still faces an active 30-day timeline to fully restore shipping lanes to full capacity.

Natural Gas: +13,800 | Δ -300 | Support $2.20 | Resist $2.65
Natural gas remains entirely cut off from the global geopolitical drama, trading in a depressed seasonal band due to robust domestic production and moderate early-summer weather across the Midwest.

Grains & Livestock (The Livestock Supply Pivot)

Corn: 91% Planted | -148,000 (Net Short) | Δ +8,000 | Current ~$4.42
The Input Relief. The sudden reopening of the Gulf drastically alters the mid-summer outlook by defusing the nitrogen fertilizer panic. Funds are maintaining a net short stance as excellent emergence numbers roll in, but commercial buyers are waiting to see if lower input costs spur late-season acreage adjustments.

Soybeans: 88% Planted | 45,000 (Net Long) | Δ -3,500 | Current ~$11.60
Funds systematically liquidated long exposure as near-perfect planting conditions across the heart of the Corn Belt completely neutralized any remaining logistical anxiety from South America.

Wheat (SRW): +85,200 (Net Long) | Δ +3,100 | Current ~$6.45
The 54-Year Deficit Floor. Wheat remains entirely insulated from the energy sell-off, with commercial grain houses fiercely defending the $6.40 baseline. The multi-decade low in domestic inventories provides a structural price floor that a macro truce cannot alter.

Live Cattle: 164,100 (Net Long) | Δ +1,200 | Cash ~$245/cwt
The Feedlot Backup. Thursday’s USDA Cattle on Feed report delivered a major reality check, showing total inventories on June 1 at 11.7 million head, a clear 2% above last year. 

While May placements plummeted a sharp 10% because early drought marketing had already pushed calves into feedyards in April, May marketings dropped a staggering 12% to the second-lowest May level since 1996. The industry is actively stacking up fat cattle supplies at a time when cow herds are at historic lows, making the record net long speculative position highly vulnerable to a sharp correction.

The Week Ahead: Critical Drivers
The Swiss Treaty Progress: Watch the wire closely for the formal implementation talks in Switzerland. If any friction emerges regarding Iran’s nuclear terms or the $300 billion reconstruction fund, energy markets will instantly gap back up.

The Post-Fed Dollar Surge: Monitor the U.S. Dollar Index response to Warsh's hawkish pivot. A sustained dollar rally will keep broad commodity indices and export-dependent ag values under heavy technical pressure.

Post-Report Cattle Action: Watch how the live cattle futures market digests the combination of higher total on-feed supplies, dismal marketing rates, and collapsing summer crude prices on Monday morning.

Pro Tip: The Livestock Trap
Do Not Chase the Long Position in Live Cattle: The combination of the June Cattle on Feed data and the macro energy crash creates a dangerous trap for livestock producers. While the 10% drop in May placements sounds bullish on the surface, the 12% plunge in marketings means feedlots are getting backed up with heavy cattle. 

With WTI plunging to $73, summer transportation costs are easing, but consumer macro anxieties from a hawkish Fed will quickly hit retail beef demand. Protect your margins now, hedge your fat cattle exposure, and treat this speculative top with extreme caution.
